senses.definition: A very rare mineral name used in scientific descriptions of ore deposits.
examples: The geologist mentioned rittingerite in his report on the silver ore samples.
Rittingerite is listed among several uncommon minerals found in the region.

senses.definition: An old military rank in cavalry units, roughly equal to a captain.
examples: The rittmaster led his squadron across the field.
As a rittmaster, he was responsible for training the mounted troops.

senses.definition: An old dialect word for the common tern, a kind of seabird.
examples: A rittock circled above the fishing boats in the bay.
The old poem mentions a rittock crying over the waves.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of an obscure verb "ritt"; used only in very rare or non‑standard contexts.
examples: This old text ritts the name in an unusual way.
In the dialect story, he ritts and rewrites the words playfully.

senses.definition: A formal ceremony with fixed actions and words, especially in a religion.
examples: The wedding ritual included traditional songs and prayers.
The priest explained each part of the ritual to the visitors.

senses.definition: An action or set of actions that you do in the same way every time, often as a habit.
examples: Her morning coffee is a small but important ritual.
Checking emails became a daily ritual at the office.

senses.definition: Done as part of a ceremony or done in the same fixed way every time.
examples: They burned incense as part of the ritual dance.
He gave a ritual apology at the start of the meeting.

senses.definition: A book in the Roman Catholic Church that describes the rituals and ceremonies to be performed by a priest.
examples: The priest consulted the rituale before beginning the ceremony.
An old Latin rituale was kept in the church library.

senses.definition: The process of making an action or behavior into a ritual, or treating it like a ritual.
examples: The ritualisation of daily tasks helped him feel more in control.
Some psychologists study the ritualisation of social behavior.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritualisation": several processes or instances where actions become ritual-like.
examples: The book describes different ritualisations of greeting across cultures.
Various ritualisations developed as the community grew.

senses.definition: To make an action into a ritual or carry it out in a fixed, ceremonial way.
examples: Families often ritualise mealtimes to create a sense of togetherness.
The company tried to ritualise its annual meeting with repeated traditions.

senses.definition: Done in a fixed, formal way that follows a set pattern, like a ritual.
examples: Their meetings became highly ritualised and predictable.
Many animal fights are ritualised displays rather than real combat.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of "ritualise": makes something into a ritual or carries it out in a ritual way.
examples: She ritualises her morning by following the same steps every day.
The community ritualises important life events.

senses.definition: The act of making something into a ritual or doing it in a fixed, ceremonial way.
examples: Ritualising daily chores can make them feel more meaningful.
The study looked at how children engage in ritualising their play.

senses.definition: Too much focus on rituals and formal ceremonies, sometimes instead of real belief or feeling.
examples: The movement was criticized for empty ritualism and a lack of compassion.
His faith was marked more by ritualism than by personal conviction.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritualism": different forms or examples of excessive attention to ritual.
examples: The article compares various ritualisms in different religious traditions.
These ritualisms developed over centuries of practice.

senses.definition: A person who strongly supports or uses religious rituals, sometimes in a very strict way.
examples: The priest was known as a ritualist who loved detailed ceremonies.
Some reformers called him a ritualist because of his focus on traditional forms.

senses.definition: Related to or following rituals, often in a fixed and formal way.
examples: They took part in a ritualistic dance during the festival.
His speech had a ritualistic quality, repeating the same phrases each year.

senses.definition: In a way that follows a ritual, or in a very fixed and formal manner.
examples: The team members ritualistically touched the sign before entering the field.
He ritualistically checked every window before going to bed.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritualist": people who strongly support or use religious rituals, sometimes in a strict way.
examples: The ritualists resisted any change to the traditional service.
Some ritualists and reformers argued heatedly about church practices.

senses.definition: Plural of a rare noun "rituality": different aspects or forms of ritual behavior.
examples: The anthropologist examined the ritualities of village life.
Modern societies show new ritualities around media and technology.

senses.definition: The quality of being like a ritual, or the pattern of rituals in a culture or situation.
examples: He studied the rituality of political events and public ceremonies.
The rituality of their meetings gave members a sense of security.

senses.definition: The process of turning actions into rituals or giving them a fixed, formal pattern.
examples: The ritualization of conflict can reduce real violence in animal groups.
We can see a ritualization of greetings in many cultures.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritualization": several processes or examples of actions becoming ritual-like.
examples: The author traces different ritualizations of public protest.
Various ritualizations have appeared in the digital world.

senses.definition: To make an action into a ritual or to give it a fixed, formal pattern.
examples: Some couples ritualize their anniversaries with special trips.
The team chose to ritualize their pre-game warm-up.

senses.definition: Having the form of a ritual or being done in a fixed, formal way.
examples: Many office tasks have become ritualized over time.
In some species, aggression takes the form of ritualized displays.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of "ritualize": makes something into a ritual or gives it a fixed pattern.
examples: She ritualizes her study sessions with the same opening routine.
The community ritualizes important milestones.

senses.definition: The act of turning something into a ritual or giving it a fixed, formal pattern.
examples: Ritualizing family time can strengthen relationships.
The therapist warned against ritualizing certain anxious behaviors.

senses.definition: Without rituals or without fixed ceremonial forms.
examples: They preferred a simple, ritualless form of worship.
The meeting was informal and almost ritualless.

senses.definition: In a way that follows a ritual or fixed pattern.
examples: The story is ritually told every year at the festival.
He ritually checked his phone before going to sleep.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritual": ceremonies or fixed patterns of behavior, especially in religion or everyday life.
examples: Different cultures have different funeral rituals.
Their family has many small rituals around meals and holidays.

senses.definition: A Latin word meaning "rite" or "ceremony", used mainly in academic or church contexts.
examples: The phrase comes from the Latin term ritus.
Scholars discussed how the ancient ritus was practiced.

senses.definition: A medicine used to treat some cancers and autoimmune diseases by targeting specific B cells in the immune system.
examples: The doctor recommended rituximab as part of her treatment plan.
Rituximab can be used for certain types of lymphoma.

senses.definition: Plural form referring to different brands or doses of the drug rituximab.
examples: Various rituximabs are now available as biosimilars.
The study compared several rituximabs across patient groups.

senses.definition: Showy style, luxury, or display meant to impress other people.
examples: The hotel lobby was full of ritz and glamour.
They added a bit of ritz to the event with live music.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritz": several examples of showy luxury or display.
examples: The magazine showed the ritzes of celebrity life.
He was tired of the ritzes and preferred a quiet evening at home.

senses.definition: More luxurious, expensive, or showy than something else.
examples: They moved to a ritzier neighborhood on the other side of town.
This restaurant is ritzier than the one we usually go to.

senses.definition: The most luxurious, expensive, or showy of a group.
examples: It was the ritziest hotel she had ever stayed in.
They chose the ritziest dress in the store for the gala.

senses.definition: In a luxurious, showy, or fancy way.
examples: The room was ritzily decorated for the party.
Guests were ritzily dressed in designer clothes.

senses.definition: The quality of being luxurious, showy, or fancy.
examples: The ritziness of the lobby impressed the visitors.
Some people enjoy the ritziness, while others find it excessive.

senses.definition: Plural of "ritziness": different kinds or examples of showy luxury.
examples: The author described the various ritzinesses of city nightlife.
Different ritzinesses appeal to different guests.

senses.definition: Luxurious, expensive, and designed to impress people, sometimes in a showy way.
examples: They stayed in a ritzy hotel near the beach.
She wore a ritzy gown to the awards ceremony.

senses.definition: In some Scandinavian languages, a colloquial word for intensity or a strong, sharp feeling, but not a standard English word.
examples: The Swedish phrase uses "riv" to describe the song's energy.
In that context, "riv" suggests a sharp or scratchy feeling.

senses.definition: An Italian word meaning the bank of a river or the seashore, sometimes used in English place names.
examples: They walked along the riva in the Italian town.
The hotel stood right on the riva overlooking the lake.

senses.definition: A literary word, borrowed from French, meaning the shore or coastline.
examples: The poet watched the waves breaking on the lonely rivage.
Ships appeared one by one along the distant rivage.

senses.definition: Plural of "rivage": several shores or coastlines, used in literary style.
examples: The novel follows the hero across many distant rivages.
Storms had reshaped the island's rivages over the years.

senses.definition: A person, team, or thing that competes with another or is as good as another.
examples: Their main rival won the championship this year.
She finally beat her biggest rival in the race.

senses.definition: To be as good as something or someone else, or to compete with them.
examples: No other singer can rival her voice.
The new phone rivals the best models on the market.

senses.definition: Competing with another person, group, or thing.
examples: The two rival gangs finally agreed to a truce.
Several rival companies bid for the contract.

senses.definition: Able to be rivaled or matched; possible to equal.
examples: His skill is hardly rivalable in this small town.
The chef produced dishes of a quality only rivalable in top restaurants.

senses.definition: Past tense of "rival": was as good as or competed with someone or something.
examples: Her performance rivaled that of professional actors.
The city's beauty rivaled any European capital.

senses.definition: A rare word for a female rival.
examples: In the novel, her main rivaless is a charming singer.
She faced a younger rivaless in the competition.

senses.definition: Plural of "rivaless": several female rivals.
examples: The story tells of two rivalesses fighting for the throne.
Her many rivalesses were secretly afraid of her talent.

senses.definition: The act of being as good as or competing with someone or something.
examples: She is rivaling more experienced runners this season.
The new product is rivaling the market leader in sales.
